title: Kafka & Messaging Systems
description: Apache Kafka and messaging systems in general: the log model, partitions, consumer groups, delivery semantics, replication and choosing a broker.
questions: What is Apache Kafka, and how does its distributed-log model differ from a traditional message broker like RabbitMQ where messages are removed once consumed? What new use cases does keeping the log open up?

Explain topics, partitions and offsets. What ordering guarantee does Kafka actually give, and how does the message key determine which partition a record lands on — with what consequence for ordering?

What is a consumer group? Explain how partitions are assigned to consumers in a group, and how the same topic can serve both a work-queue pattern (competing consumers) and a pub/sub pattern (multiple groups).

Compare the delivery semantics in messaging systems: at-most-once, at-least-once and exactly-once. What do the producer's `acks` setting, the idempotent producer and Kafka transactions each contribute?
How does consumer offset management work? Compare auto-commit with manual commit (before vs after processing), and explain how each choice maps to message loss vs duplicate processing after a crash.
Explain Kafka replication: partition leaders and followers, the in-sync replica (ISR) set, and how `acks=all` together with `min.insync.replicas` prevents data loss when a broker fails.
Compare time/size-based retention with log compaction. How does a compacted topic behave, and when would you use one (e.g. changelog, event sourcing, restoring state)?

What goes wrong operationally with partitions? Discuss choosing a partition count (and why increasing it later breaks key ordering), hot partitions caused by skewed keys, and what a consumer-group rebalance does to processing.

What is consumer lag, why does it matter, and how do you deal with a consumer group that can't keep up? Discuss scaling limits (consumers vs partitions), batch tuning and backpressure in log-based vs push-based brokers.

You need asynchronous communication between services: when would you pick Kafka, when a queue broker like RabbitMQ, and when neither (just synchronous calls)? Compare them on throughput, ordering, routing flexibility, replay and operational cost.

Explain Kafka transactions and the exactly-once processing (EOS) model in a consume-transform-produce pipeline: what do the transactional producer, `isolation.level=read_committed` and offset commits inside the transaction each contribute — and what does EOS still NOT protect (side effects like DB writes or emails)?

What happens during a consumer group rebalance? Compare eager (stop-the-world) with cooperative/incremental rebalancing, explain what static group membership is for, and describe the symptoms of a rebalance storm caused by slow processing and `max.poll.interval.ms`.

Your consumer processes a record, writes to PostgreSQL, and then crashes before committing the offset. Walk through what happens on restart, why this forces idempotent processing, and compare deduplication strategies: idempotency keys in the DB, upserts, and storing processed offsets in the same transaction as the data.

How do you handle poison messages and retries in Kafka, where there is no built-in dead-letter mechanism like RabbitMQ's DLX? Compare blocking retries in the consumer, retry topics with backoff (the pattern behind Spring/Micronaut retry topics), and a dead-letter topic — including what each does to ordering guarantees.

questions.question: Which statements about Kafka partitions and ordering are true?
options: Kafka guarantees message order within a single partition
Kafka guarantees message order across the whole topic
Records with the same key always land on the same partition (with unchanged partition count)
Two consumers in the same group can read the same partition simultaneously

questions.question: Which statements about consumer groups are true?
options: Within a group, each partition is consumed by at most one consumer
Multiple independent groups each receive every message of the topic
Adding more consumers than partitions increases the group's throughput
Committed offsets are tracked per consumer group

questions.question: Which statements about the producer acks setting are true?
options: acks=all waits until all in-sync replicas have the record
acks=0 sends without waiting for any broker acknowledgement
acks=1 waits only for the partition leader
acks=all by itself guarantees no data loss, regardless of min.insync.replicas

questions.question: A consumer commits its offsets only AFTER successfully processing each batch. Which delivery semantics does this give?
options: At-most-once
At-least-once
Exactly-once
No delivery guarantee at all

questions.question: Replication factor 3, min.insync.replicas=2, producer uses acks=all. Which statements are true?
options: The setup keeps accepting writes with one broker down
The producer receives an error when the ISR shrinks below 2
min.insync.replicas controls how many replicas the partition has in total
A follower that falls too far behind is removed from the ISR

questions.question: Which statements about log compaction are true?
options: A compacted topic retains at least the latest value for every key
Compaction deletes all messages older than the retention window regardless of key
A record with a null value (tombstone) marks its key for deletion
Compacted topics suit changelogs and restoring state after a restart

questions.question: Which statements about consumer group rebalances are true?
options: A rebalance is triggered when a consumer joins or leaves the group
Eager rebalancing stops consumption of all partitions until reassignment finishes
Cooperative (incremental) rebalancing only moves the partitions that change owner
After a rebalance the group re-reads the topic from the beginning

questions.question: What is consumer lag?
options: The difference between the latest produced offset and the consumer's position in the partition
The number of consumers missing from the group relative to the partition count
The time elapsed since the consumer's last poll() call
The disk size of the topic's uncompacted segments

questions.question: Which statements about exactly-once semantics (EOS) in Kafka are true?
options: It requires a transactional producer and consumers reading with isolation.level=read_committed
It also protects side effects like database writes or sent emails
The idempotent producer eliminates duplicates caused by producer retries
It covers consume-transform-produce pipelines where input offsets are committed inside the transaction

questions.question: Which statements about partition-count operations are true?
options: Increasing the partition count later changes key-to-partition mapping and breaks per-key ordering
The partition count of an existing topic cannot be changed at all
Hot partitions are typically caused by skewed message keys
Adding partitions is always safe because Kafka rehashes old data automatically
